source_metadata.title_raw: David A. Schwarz. Esq - A-19-169 - Oct 15, 2019 - Newport Beach

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
David A. Schwarz, Esq. 
Irell & Manella LLP 
840 Newport Center Drive 
Newport Beach, CA 92660-6324 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-169 
Dear Mr. Schwarz: 
October 15, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Rancho Santiago Community 
College President Phillip Yarbrough, Ramona Unified School District Board Member Rodger 
Dohm, Los Alamitos Mayor Warren Kusumoto, Rossmoor Community Service District Director 
Jeffrey I. Barke, Mission Viejo Councilmember Ed Sachs, San Clemente Councilmember Laura 
Ferguson, Capistrano Unified School District Board Member Jim Reardon, and Whittier Unified 
School District Board Member Leighton Anderson regarding the gift and campaign provisions of 
the Political Reform Act ("Act"). 1 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate .. Ifthis is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Are pro bona or discounted legal services, or the payment of such discounted legal services 
by a third party, rendered in connection with a constitutional challenge to a state statute considered 
gifts or contributions to President Yarborough, Board Member Dohm, Mayor Kusumoto, Director 
Barke, Councilmember Sachs, Councilmember Ferguson, Board Member Reardon, and Board 
Member Anderson ("Clients")? 
CONCLUSION 
The value of pro bona services to these public officials is neither considered a gift nor a 
contribution. However, there may be behested payment reporting obligations for these officials. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


t' 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-169 
Page No. 2 
Your law firm Irell & Manella LLP ("Irell"), as co-counsel with the non-profit public 
interest law firm, Center for Individual Rights ("CIR"), and the Law Office of Mark W. Bucher, 
intends to represent the Clients in a constitutional challenge to California Government Code Section 
3550. The statute directly impacts the ability of a public employer to communicate with, or respond 
to, employees, constituents, and members of the public concerning issues relating to unionization. 
After the California Policy Center became aware of CIR's interest in bringing a 
constitutional challenge to the statute, it put CIR in touch with individuals interested in 
participating. 
After CIR reached out to another firm regarding the potential litigation, CIR contacted your 
firm. Irell was already providing legal representation concerning Section 3550 in a matter pending 
before the California Public Employees Retirement Board. 
You summarize the possible impacts of the litigation as follows. If the challenge succeeds, a 
court will declare some or all aspects of the statute unconstitutional and enjoin further enforcement 
of the statute against all public officials - not just the Clients. If the Clients are unsuccessful, they, 
along with all public employees in this state, will be provided the needed clarity to understand the 
limits on their ability to speak publicly on issues of public concern. Regardless of the outcome, the 
Clients, and any other similarly-situated public officials, will benefit from a judicial declaration. 
Thus, the outcome of this litigation would inure to the benefit of the public as well as those officials 
who represent their constituents in matters implicated by the law at issue. 
CIR wants to provide pro bono representation to the Clients because of the First 
Amendment interests at stake, not only for each Client but for the other public employers, 
employees, and constituents affected by the statute. The decision to pursue such a challenge does 
not depend on the identity of the plaintiff; all public employees have a similar if not equal interest 
in the outcome of this litigation. CIR does not conduct business before any of the public entities 
represented by these Clients. Likewise, Irell has no pending matters before the Clients' public 
agencies that might bear on the firm's financial or other business interests. 
CIR contacted Mark W. Bucher to serve as local counsel. Mr. Bucher agreed to do so pro 
bono because of the public interest issues implicated. Mr. Bucher has no pending business matters 
before the Clients' public agencies. 
Gifts: 
ANALYSIS 
A gift is defined under Section 82028(a) as: 
[A]ny payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that 
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or discount in 
the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of 
business to members of the public without regard to official status. 


File No. A-19-169 
Page No. 3 
To reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of gifts by 
local public officials in three ways: 
• 
Gift Limit of $500: The Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public 
officials. (Section 89503.) The current limit is $500 from a single source in a calendar year. 
(Regulation 18940.2.) 
• 
Gift Reporting: In order that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, 
the Act imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that any gift of 
$50 or more (or gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during 
the calendar year are disclosed on the officials' statement of economic interests. (Section 
87207.) The term "income" includes any gift. (Section 82030.) 
• 
Conflict of Interest Disqualification: The Act prohibits any public official from making, 
participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence the outcome of a 
governmental decision involving the donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $500 
or more provided to, received by, or promised to the official within the ·12 months prior to 
the date the decision is made. (Sections 87100 and 87103(e).) 
Under Section 82028, anything of value given to a public official without cost, including 
legal services, may be a reportable gift, unless it is expressly exempted from the definition of "gift." 
(Connor Advice Letter, No. A-94-247; Dorsey Advice Letter, No. 1-92-302.) Therefore, pro bono 
legal services may constitute "gifts," which can subject an official to the disqualification provisions 
of the Act. 
If pro bono or discounted legal services were provided to the Clients to challenge the 
constitutionality of this statute, it appears that the services would not convey a personal benefit on 
any single Client. As you stated, the potential litigation implicates all public officials in the state, 
and the benefit of the challenge could inure to the public, to a lesser extent. Accordingly, the legal 
services would not be a gift to the Clients. 
Contributions and Behested Payment : 
Section 82015 defines a "contribution" as any payment, forgiveness of a loan, a payment of 
a loan by a third party, or ·an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that full 
and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it 
is not made for political purposes. A payment is made.for political purposes if it is: (1) for the 
purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against the 
nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure; 
or, (2) it is received by or made at the behest of a candidate. (Regulation 18215(a).) 
A "payment" means a "payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other 
rendering of money, property services or anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible." 
(Section 82044.) A payment is "made at the behest of' when "made under the control or at the 
direction of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with, at the request or suggestion 
of, or with the express prior consent of." (Section 82041.3.) 


File No. A-19-169 
Page No. 4 
The value of pro bona or discounted legal services constitutes a "payment," which may be 
classified as a "contribution" when made to an elected official for a political purpose. Personal 
services are tendered for political purposes if they are carried on for the purpose of influencing or 
attempting to influence the actions of the voters for or against the election of one or more 
candidates. The facts provided do not indicate the services to the Clients are for the purpose of 
influencing the actions of voters. That being the case, the pro bono or discounted legal services you 
described are not a contribution under the Act pursuant to Section .82015 and Regulation 18215. 
However, because the pro bono or discounted legal services are provided principally for a 
governmental purpose, behested payments reporting may apply. The Act presumes that payments 
made "principally for a legislative, governmental, or charitable purpose" are neither gifts nor 
contributions. (Section 84004.5(c)(4)-(5).) 
Section 84224(a) states that if an individual or entity, at the behest of an elected officer, 
makes monetary or in-kind donations totaling $5,000 or more in a calendar year for a legislative, 
governmental, or charitable purpose, that officer has to file a report detailing those donations with 
his or her public agency within 30 days after reaching the $5,000 threshold and each 30 days 
thereafter for additional donations by the same donor. The report is filed on Form 803, entitled 
"Behested Payment Report." Thus, since the legal services are related to governmental business, to 
the extent that a person provides services worth $5,000 or more, the Clients will be required to file 
behested payment reports. If the donated legal services total $5,000 or more, the Clients must 
account for and report the full value of the legal services on his or her Behested Payment Report 
(Form 803). 
If you have ~ther questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
RPOC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Ryan O'Connor 
Counsel, Legal Division
